blocks.heading: Big projects need a plan. 🗺️
blocks.html:
Making a digital product takes many steps.

A design process is a set of steps you follow in order.

blocks.heading: There are 4 stages in the design process. 🔢
blocks.html:
1. Investigate and plan
2. Design and draft
3. Produce
4. Evaluate

You do them in this order, one at a time.

blocks.heading: Stage 1: Investigate and plan. 🔍
blocks.html:
Find out who will use your product. This is your target audience.

Ask: what do they need? What do they already have?

blocks.heading: Stage 2: Design and draft. ✏️
blocks.html:
Sketch your ideas before you build anything.

A draft could be a drawing, a storyboard, or a simple plan on paper.

blocks.heading: Stage 3: Produce. 🛠️
blocks.html:
Now you actually make the digital product.

You follow your draft from Stage 2 as you build it.

blocks.heading: Stage 4: Evaluate. ✅
blocks.html:
Check your finished product against your plan.

Ask: does it do what the target audience needs?

blocks.title: Put the 4 design process stages in the correct order. 🔢

options: The people who will use your product
The software you use to build it
question.explanation: The target audience is the group of people your product is made for. You plan around their needs.

question.text: Which stage comes first in the design process? 🔍
question.explanation: You always investigate and plan first. This helps you understand the task before you design or build anything.

question.text: What happens in the Evaluate stage? ✅
options: You check the product against your plan
question.explanation: Evaluate means checking your finished work. You ask if it meets the plan and the needs of the target audience.

blocks.heading: Big projects are often done in teams. 👥
blocks.html:
One person cannot do everything alone.

Each team member gets a different role, or job, to do.

blocks.heading: There are 4 main roles in a project team. 🧑‍🤝‍🧑
blocks.html:
Project manager
Researcher
Media coordinator
Content writer

Each role has its own job to do.

blocks.heading: The project manager keeps the team on track. 📋
blocks.html:
They watch the timeline and check deadlines.

They make sure every team member knows their job.

blocks.heading: The researcher finds information. 🔎
blocks.html:
They look up facts the team needs.

They check that information is true and correct.

blocks.heading: The media coordinator collects images and sound. 🎬
blocks.html:
They find or make photos, video, and audio.

They check that media files are the right type and size.

blocks.heading: The content writer writes the words. ✍️
blocks.html:
They write text for the product, like captions or pages.

They check the writing is clear and easy to read.

blocks.title: Match each role to what they do. 🧩

pairs.definition: Keeps the team on track and checks deadlines 📋
Writes the words for the product ✍️

question.text: Who checks that the team meets its deadlines? 📋
question.explanation: The project manager keeps the team on track. They watch the timeline and check deadlines.

question.text: Which role collects photos and video for the project? 🎬
question.explanation: The media coordinator collects images, video, and sound. They check the files are the right type and size.

question.text: What does the content writer do? ✍️
options: Writes the words for the product
question.explanation: The content writer writes the text used in the product, such as captions or page text.

blocks.heading: Teams need rules to work well together. 🤝
blocks.html:
These rules are called group work protocols.

They help everyone know what to do and how to act.

blocks.heading: Strategy 1: Set group work protocols. 📜
blocks.html:
Agree on rules at the start, like meeting times.

Example rule: everyone listens before speaking.

blocks.heading: Strategy 2: Clearly identify roles. 🏷️
blocks.html:
Everyone should know their own job.

This stops two people doing the same task, or no one doing it.

blocks.heading: Strategy 3: Communicate well. 💬
blocks.html:
Talk often and tell others how your work is going.

Ask for help early if you are stuck.

blocks.heading: Strategy 4: Set clear goals and timelines. ⏰
blocks.html:
A goal is what you want to finish.
A timeline is when each part is due.

Clear goals and timelines help the team stay on track.

blocks.heading: Good teamwork makes better projects. 🌟
blocks.html:
When everyone does their role and talks often, the project goes smoothly.

Poor teamwork can make a project late or messy.

blocks.title: Sort each statement into the correct column. 📊

categories.label: Good teamwork ✅
items: The team has a clear timeline

categories.label: Poor teamwork ⚠️
items: No one knows who does what
Team members never talk to each other
There is no deadline for any task

blocks.title: Complete the sentence: A ___ is when each part of the project is due.
blocks.explanation: A timeline shows when each part of the project must be finished by.

options: The final mark for the project
Rules the team agrees on for how to work together
A list of software to use
question.explanation: Group work protocols are rules the team sets at the start, like meeting times and how to communicate.

question.text: Why is it important to clearly identify roles? 🏷️
options: So the team can skip the evaluate stage
So everyone knows their own job and nothing is missed
So only one person needs to work
question.explanation: Clear roles stop two people doing the same task, or a task being missed completely.

question.text: What helps a team know when each task is due? ⏰
question.explanation: A timeline sets clear dates for when each part of the project should be finished.
